lessons.note: Add **-기 시작하다** to a verb stem to say an action begins. The 시작하다 part carries the tense, so the past is **-기 시작했어요**.

sentences.english: It started to rain.
I started to study Korean.
The baby started to cry.
I start work at nine.
The students started to laugh.

lessons.meaning: to come to / to end up / to get to (do something)
lessons.example: 한국 친구를 만나서 한국어를 잘하게 됐어요.
lessons.example_en: I met a Korean friend, so I got to be good at Korean.
lessons.note: **-게 되다** shows a change that happens through circumstances rather than your own plan. The past **됐어요** is a contraction of 되었어요.

sentences.english: I got to like Korean food.
I came to live in Seoul.
I ended up knowing the truth.
I got to meet a famous singer.
I will end up going to Korea next year.

lessons.meaning: to be in a resulting state (after the action finished)
lessons.example_en: Grandmother is sitting on the chair.
lessons.note: Use **-아/어 있다** for the state that remains after an action, mostly with intransitive verbs like 앉다, 서다, 열리다. This differs from **-고 있다**, which is the ongoing action.

sentences.english: Grandmother is sitting on the chair.
A lot of people are standing in front of the store.
accepted: 가게 앞에 많은 사람들이 서 있어요.

sentences.english: The window is open.
My name is written on the paper.
The light is on in the room.

lessons.meaning: to look / to seem (a certain way)
lessons.note: Attach **-아/어 보이다** to an adjective stem to describe how something looks. Mind the contractions: 예쁘다 → **예뻐 보여요**, 좋다 → **좋아 보여요**.

sentences.english: You look tired today.
This bag looks expensive.
The food looks delicious.
She looks young.
Today you look happy.

lessons.example_en: It seems to be raining outside.
lessons.note: Use **-는 것 같다** with verbs and **-(으)ㄴ 것 같다** with adjectives to make a soft guess. For a past guess use **-(으)ㄴ 것 같다** on a verb.

sentences.english: It seems to be raining outside.
This movie seems interesting.
I think this place is good.
I think she already left.
sentences.korean: 그 사람은 벌써 간 것 같아요.
accepted: 그 사람은 벌써 간 것 같아요.
그 사람은 벌써 간 것 같습니다.
그 사람이 벌써 간 것 같아요.

sentences.english: The weather seems cold today.

lessons.example_en: I decided to meet my friend tomorrow.
lessons.note: Add **-기로 하다** to a verb stem to say you have decided or arranged to do it. Because the decision is already made, it is usually **-기로 했어요**.

sentences.english: I decided to meet my friend tomorrow.
I decided to exercise every day.
We decided to study together.
I decided to quit smoking.
I decided not to eat late at night.

lessons.example_en: Because it is raining, I am staying home.
lessons.note: Add **-기 때문에** to a verb or adjective stem to give a strong reason. After a noun use **N이기 때문에 / N 때문에**. The result clause carries the tense.

sentences.english: Because it is raining, I am staying home.
Because I am busy, I cannot go.
Because the weather is cold, I wear a coat.
Because I like Korean food, I often eat it.
sentences.korean: 한국 음식을 좋아하기 때문에 자주 먹어요.
accepted: 한국 음식을 좋아하기 때문에 자주 먹어요.
한국 음식을 좋아하기 때문에 자주 먹습니다.
저는 한국 음식을 좋아하기 때문에 자주 먹어요.

sentences.english: Because there was a lot of work, I was tired.

lessons.meaning: I wish / it would be nice if
lessons.example_en: I hope the weather is nice tomorrow.
lessons.note: Attach **-(으)면 좋겠다** to a stem to express a wish: **-면** after a vowel, **-으면** after a consonant. The polite form is **좋겠어요**.

sentences.english: I hope the weather is nice tomorrow.
I wish it would not rain.
I wish I could pass the exam.
It would be nice if you came too.
I wish I had more time.
